data.title: "The New Era of Tokens": Ten Questions and Answers about China's AI Industry
data.description:
The Chinese AI basic model industry is at a critical juncture, shifting from "expectation-driven" to "demand-driven." A recent research report from JPMorgan Chase systematically answered ten core questions investors had about the industry, concluding that model quality has become the primary variable determining the market landscape, and industry differentiation will accelerate. According to the report released by JPMorgan Chase on March 27, the report points out that the Chinese AI market is at a clear inflection point, with demand growth in coding and intelligent agent scenarios accelerating. Domestic model capabilities have approached or even surpassed the level of leading US models a year ago, and local pricing is more economically commensurate with performance, both factors improving the return on investment. 2026 will be a crucial year for Chinese companies' AI demand to replicate the growth curve seen in the US in 2025. Using Anthropic as a benchmark, its annual recurring revenue (ARR) increased from $1 billion in December 2024 to $19 billion in March 2026, a growth of approximately 19 times in 15 months. The Chinese market has the potential to follow a similar path, especially in the coding field, where internet giants such as Tencent, Alibaba, and ByteDance have integrated relevant tools into their existing ecosystems, driving demand from standalone demonstrations to full-scale deployment. The bank maintains its "Overweight" rating on Zhipu and MiniMax, with target prices of HK$800 and HK$1100 respectively. Question 1: Is AI demand growing linearly or experiencing an inflection point explosion? Demand is driven by an inflection point, not linearly. Once the model quality is high enough to unlock real-world applications, usage will shift from linear growth to a "convex curve"-like explosion. The strongest evidence comes from the US market: Anthropic's annual recurring revenue (ARR) surged from $1 billion in December 2024 to $19 billion in March 2026 in just 15 months, a nearly 19-fold increase. China currently possesses the fundamental conditions for a similar explosion: domestic model capabilities have surpassed the level of leading US models a year ago, and local pricing is more in line with China's economic efficiency for artificial intelligence. These two factors combined significantly improve the expected return on investment for AI implementation. On the intelligent agent side, OpenClaw has become a crucial catalyst, pushing usage scenarios from single-turn interactions to multi-step task execution, significantly increasing the number of tokens consumed per task. Internet giants such as Tencent, Alibaba, and ByteDance have integrated OpenClaw-related tools into their existing ecosystems, marking the evolution of the trend from "developer experimentation" to "comprehensive ecosystem deployment." Question 2: Will API pricing rise, fall, or differentiate? Pricing will not move in one direction; differentiation is the main theme. On the one hand, powerful models gain pricing power. If a model can uniquely unlock high-value tasks (agent coding, long-duration workflows, enterprise-level reliability), customers are willing to pay a premium because the returns are quantifiable. On the other hand, as hardware and algorithm efficiency continue to improve, the unit cost of inference will continue to decline, putting price pressure on models whose capabilities have stagnated. The end result is a differentiated pricing structure: models that consistently maintain cutting-edge capabilities can achieve both increased volume and price; models that fail to iterate continuously will face price declines, and even if usage continues to grow, profit margins will become uncertain. Question 3: If pricing is not the main battleground, where is the focus of competition? The main battleground has shifted from token prices to model capabilities. This is a key change compared to last year—the focus of the Chinese market in 2025 will be a full-blown price war, while in today's fastest-growing coding and intelligent agent scenarios, quality is far more important than unit price. In multi-step workflows, the essence of customer purchases is not "cheap tokens," but "successful task completion." The research report provides an intuitive mathematical example: if the success rate of a single step increases from 85% to 98%, the final completion rate of a 20-step task will jump from 4% to 67%. Under this logic, the model with the lowest price per token may actually have the highest actual overall cost to complete each task. The report also points out that companies with strong, cutting-edge models can easily extend into the low-end market, but companies that rely solely on low prices will find it difficult to move into the high-end market. Question 4: Why is the basic large-scale model industry still a "life-or-death struggle" industry? Small technological gaps, endless iteration cycles, and converging monetization models—these three factors determine the industry's extreme brutality. The capability gap between large-scale model companies in China is often smaller than investors anticipate, leading to high market instability. In this industry, "standing still" is not a neutral outcome but signifies a loss of status—companies must continuously invest and iterate to avoid falling behind. The convergence of business models exacerbates the pressure of elimination. Revenue growth and profit margins primarily depend on product strength, and switching costs remain low. This means that companies losing technological momentum will quickly lose their commercial and financial resilience, and the number of truly reliable companies in the industry will gradually decrease. Question 5: What are the determinants of profitability? The core issue is whether gross profit growth can consistently outpace R&D expenditure growth. The basic economic model for the token business is clear: Revenue = token usage × price. The main cost is inference computation, and the largest operating expense is training-related R&D. As model efficiency and inference chip efficiency continue to improve, the gross profit margin of cutting-edge models should gradually increase. However, the operating profit outlook is more complex. Anthropic serves as a cautionary tale: even with monthly revenue reaching $14 billion in February 2026, the company still announced a new $30 billion funding round during the same period, emphasizing continued cutting-edge development—high revenue does not necessarily mean normalized training intensity. The baseline scenario is that both Zhipu and MiniMax are expected to turn a profit starting in 2029. The research report emphasizes that more important tracking indicators than specific profit years are: a sustained growth trend in usage and a sustained improvement in unit economics. Question Six: How should investors track model performance? It is necessary to combine three dimensions: token price, usage, and third-party evaluation. A single indicator is insufficient to explain the situation. Token Price: This is the most important metric because it's a real-time expression of a company's market positioning for its product. The price difference with the best model is becoming a good proxy variable for the model's actual competitiveness. Token Usage: Actual consumption reflects the true choices of users and developers. Third-party API aggregators like OpenRouter can be used as a reference, especially focusing on the growth of agent-type workloads, as these workloads consume far more tokens per task than simple workflows. Third-Party Evaluation: Artificial Analysis provides structured evaluation, while LMARaena reflects the blind selection preferences of real users. The two complement each other, forming a more complete external perspective.

Question 7: Internet giants are making a major foray into the B2B market; what will become of independent model companies?

Competitive boundaries are converging, and ultimately it still comes down to a competition of model capabilities.

Alibaba has clearly identified cloud and AI as strategic priorities, deeply integrating model development with enterprise workflows; Tencent's intelligent agent products have covered all scenarios for individuals, developers, and enterprises; OpenAI is also shifting its commercialization focus to enterprise products and code deployment. Leading companies are all heading in the same direction: AI is evolving from a "consumer-end function" to a "tool that directly generates enterprise revenue."

In this context, independent model companies can no longer form a competitive advantage simply by virtue of their "cloud neutrality," and internet giants cannot fully compensate for the shortcomings of model capabilities with just their ecosystem traffic advantages. When deploying AI, enterprise clients still prioritize model quality—stronger coding and inference capabilities, and more reliable workflow completion rates. Question 8: What factors determine a company's survival? Talent is first, computing power is second, and organization is third; all three are indispensable. Top research talent: This remains a research-driven industry. The technical judgment of senior management is itself a competitive factor; whether management can make correct research direction decisions directly affects the company's technological trajectory. Computing power and capital: Cutting-edge training costs are high, and the economics of inference depend on the quality of infrastructure. Weak computing power acquisition capabilities are a structural disadvantage—not only affecting model training efficiency but also weakening the ability to respond to demand at a reasonable cost. Organizational execution: In a rapidly iterating market, the ability to transform research results into products, products into usage, and usage into monetization is almost as important as the model itself. Question 9: If everyone is making progress, will models eventually converge? Overall strength will converge, but not; the market will not form a winner-takes-all pattern. Different companies differ in architecture selection, training data, product focus, and technical paths, and these differences will continue to generate different competitive advantages. The research report argues that in a rapidly expanding market, multiple companies can grow simultaneously, even with some overlapping capabilities—the significance of overall market expansion at this stage far outweighs premature concerns about commoditization. In the long run, a more realistic market outcome is not "one dominant player and the rest eliminated," but rather a few truly strong companies, each with their own strengths, competing in a market capable of supporting multiple winners. As AI expands from productivity tools to consumer scenarios, differences in personal tastes, styles, and preferences will further reinforce this diversified landscape. Question 10: How to unify the understanding of open-source/closed-source, model iteration, and global expansion risks? Iteration is a necessity, open-source/closed-source is a strategic choice, and the core risks of global expansion lie in computing power and compliance. Regarding model iteration, the expected pace is approximately one flagship model per year (e.g., from GLM 4.7 to GLM 5, from the MiniMax M2 series to the M3 series), accompanied by minor upgrades driven by reinforcement learning. Stopping iteration means losing a competitive position. Regarding open-source/closed-source models, the research report argues that the answer is not either/or. Closed-source models offer stronger commercial defense and reduce the risk of disintermediation; open-source models, on the other hand, facilitate ecosystem building, increase adoption rates, and accelerate technology feedback. Therefore, most Chinese model companies will ultimately adopt a hybrid strategy: using the latest and most powerful closed-source models, and open-sourcing other versions. In terms of global expansion, the biggest risk remains access to computing power. Training and inference are highly dependent on high-performance chips, and tightening export controls will simultaneously weaken the speed of model improvement and cost competitiveness. Secondly, there are data and security compliance risks: if model deployment, user services, and data storage can be localized overseas, cross-border data transfer issues are relatively controllable; however, local privacy regulations and the determination of data access permissions for Chinese-related entities remain sources of uncertainty.

data.title: A sober reflection on compliance amid the RWA craze in Hong Kong
data.description:
I. Introduction

On February 13, Esperanza Securities received approval from the Hong Kong Securities and Futures Commission to conduct entertainment asset tokenization (tokenization) business on the box office revenue rights of Wong Kai-Ching's 40th Anniversary Hong Kong Coliseum Concert. On February 24, the RWA project of Delin Holdings' Central Delin Building real estate was approved for issuance. At the beginning of 2026, prime commercial real estate in Hong Kong's Central core business district, as well as the tickets and revenue rights of the "Hong Kong Coliseum" concert, hailed as a benchmark of Asian pop culture, were successively "on-chain" in a short period of time, becoming the first RWA projects to be carried out in Hong Kong in their respective fields. This phenomenon signifies that RWA (Real World Assets) is gradually moving from the proof-of-concept (PoC) stage to a new stage of commercialization and large-scale implementation.

Hong Kong, with its unique "One Country, Two Systems" advantages, well-developed common law system, and forward-looking regulatory framework, is becoming a crucial hub for global RWA (Responsible Asset Waiver) assets. Compared to traditional lending and IPOs, issuing RWAs in Hong Kong is not only a strategic choice to broaden financing channels and reduce financing costs, but also an important path to achieve global asset pricing and liquidity release. However, behind financial innovation often lie complex legal and tax compliance risks. RWAs are not simply "old wine in new bottles"; they require issuers to establish a comprehensive compliance system across multiple dimensions, including underlying asset transparency, smart contract auditing, cross-border data compliance, and anti-money laundering (AML). This article will systematically trace the evolution and mainstream paths of Hong Kong RWAs from a professional perspective, analyze the tax implications and regulatory logic, and examine the compliance risks behind the financing benefits of RWAs. II. From Standardized Assets to Real Estate and Entertainment: The Evolution of Hong Kong RWA Looking back at the development history of Hong Kong RWA, a clear trend of evolution from standardized financial products to broader real-world assets can be seen. This not only reflects the deepening integration of blockchain technology and financial engineering, but also reflects the governance wisdom of Hong Kong regulators in seeking progress while maintaining stability. Phase One: Trial of Standardized Assets Backed by Credit (2022-2023) The start of Hong Kong RWA was not initiated by grassroots individuals, but rather promoted from the top down by the government. In February 2023, the Hong Kong SAR government successfully issued HK$800 million in tokenized green bonds (Project Evergreen), which was among the first tokenized green bonds issued by a government globally (HKMA, 2023). The characteristics of this phase are highly standardized underlying assets (bonds), high credit ratings (sovereign credit), and limited participants (institutional investors only). The core purpose is to test the cost reduction and efficiency improvement capabilities of distributed ledger technology (DLT) in traditional financial infrastructure (such as clearing and settlement). **Phase Two: On-chain Democracy of Private Lending and Fund Units (Mid-to-Late 2023)** With the initial verification of the infrastructure, the Hong Kong Securities and Futures Commission (SFC) successively issued several circulars regarding intermediaries engaging in tokenized securities activities and the SFC's approval of tokenized investment products (SFC, 2023a; SFC, 2023b). The market began to turn its attention to interest-bearing assets such as private lending and money market funds (such as tokenized US Treasuries). In this phase, RWA acted as a bridge connecting Web3 funds and traditional US dollar interest-bearing assets, opening up a channel for on-chain funds to obtain risk-free returns. Phase Three: Value Reshaping of Non-Standard Assets (Real Estate, Entertainment IP) (2026) In early 2026, the first batch of real estate and entertainment RWA projects were approved by the Hong Kong Securities and Futures Commission, further expanding the application scenarios of RWA in Hong Kong. Commercial real estate itself has a high capital threshold and low liquidity. Through RWA fractionalization, professional investors can participate in its investment and dividends at a relatively low threshold. At the same time, the cash flow of entertainment assets such as concerts and film copyrights often has obvious cyclicality and uncertainty. Putting box office revenue rights on the blockchain can combine consumption and investment behavior. Fans are both consumers and ecosystem investors. This dual attribute of "finance + consumption" greatly broadens the early financing channels of the entertainment industry. In summary, the core commercial value of RWA has gone beyond optimizing payment and settlement efficiency, and is more about releasing liquidity premiums. Assets that are illiquid and difficult to divide in the traditional financial system often have more significant appreciation potential under the RWA framework.

III. How to "put real-world assets on the blockchain"? Deconstructing the entire RWA process

(I) Case Study: Taking the RWA of Delin Building in Central as an example

In February 2026, Delin Holdings (1709.HK) disclosed that its plan to tokenize the equity of the limited partnership fund (i.e., "Delin Building LPF") holding commercial properties in Central, Hong Kong, had received regulatory approval from the Hong Kong Securities and Futures Commission with "no further comments," allowing licensed institutions to proceed with the distribution to qualified professional investors (PIs).

As a model of the integration of traditional commercial real estate and digital finance, the implementation process of such projects typically includes the following steps: 1. Asset Separation and SPV Establishment: The issuer first needs to establish a special purpose vehicle (SPV) in Hong Kong or offshore, legally transferring the ownership or revenue rights of the commercial property to the SPV. The core purpose of this step is to isolate the assets from the credit risk of the developer's parent company. 2. Third-Party Due Diligence and Valuation: A professional institution is hired to conduct financial and legal due diligence on the SPV and its underlying property, and an independent valuer issues a property valuation report to ensure that the token price has a fair basis. 3. Oracle Mechanism Design: The value and rental income of commercial real estate occur off-chain. The project needs to introduce a reliable "oracle" to periodically synchronize real-world rental data, the latest valuation, and other data to the blockchain, ensuring that token holders obtain accurate information. 4. Smart Contract Development and Auditing: The asset's dividend logic (e.g., quarterly rent distribution) and transfer restrictions (e.g., limited to Hong Kong compliant PI transactions) will be written into smart contracts. A security audit must be conducted in accordance with the law before contract deployment. 5. Token Minting and Issuance: Security tokens representing SPV equity or debt will be minted on Ethereum or other public/consortium blockchains. Each token represents a specific proportion of the fund's economic returns and voting rights. 6. Licensed Institution Distribution and Secondary Market Trading: The tokens will be underwritten and distributed by financial institutions holding Hong Kong Type 1 (Securities Dealing) and Type 4 (Advising on Securities) licenses and approved by the SFC to engage in virtual asset business. In the future, they can circulate in the compliant VATP secondary market. (II) Introduction to Mainstream Paths of RWA in Hong Kong Based on the nature and legal structure of the underlying assets, RWA in Hong Kong is currently divided into three main paths. The impact of different paths on compliance and taxation varies. (III) Corresponding Regulatory Requirements The regulatory logic of the Hong Kong Securities and Futures Commission (SFC) for RWA can be summarized in twelve words: "Same business, same risks, same rules." This means that regulators will not lower their guard simply because assets are disguised as blockchain devices, but will adopt a penetrating regulatory approach to directly address the economic substance of the underlying assets. First, regarding asset classification, according to the SFC's circular on tokenized SFC-approved investment products issued in November 2023, if the underlying assets of RWA are traditional stocks, bonds, or fund units, the corresponding tokens will be classified as "tokenized securities." The issuance of such products is, in principle, subject to the Hong Kong Securities and Futures Ordinance's requirements for the issuance, disclosure, and licensing of traditional securities. Second, for non-standard assets, if their tokenization structure embeds complex derivative logic or special transfer restrictions, the SFC tends to consider them "complex products." This requires distribution institutions (brokerages/banks) to conduct appropriate suitability assessments when recommending them to clients, limiting subscriptions to professional investors, such as professional investment individuals holding more than HK$8 million in liquid assets. In terms of technical and operational compliance, regulators typically require issuers to conduct independent third-party audits of smart contracts to prevent asset risks caused by code vulnerabilities or hacker attacks from the technical source. Simultaneously, regarding anti-money laundering, issuers and virtual asset trading platforms must fully implement KYC procedures and strictly adhere to the Travel Rule to ensure the transparent and traceable identities of the sender and receiver of every transaction on the blockchain. (IV) Relevant Tax Impacts Path One (Asset-Backed Tokens): This path primarily involves profits tax. If the issuer directly sells tokens representing local Hong Kong physical assets (such as machinery and equipment), this activity can be considered a sale occurring in Hong Kong. According to Hong Kong's Inland Revenue Ordinance, profits generated from such transactions are subject to profits tax, generally at a standard rate of 15% or 16.5% depending on the type of entity. Path Two (Fund Share Tokens): At the fund and token level, tax treatment is more complex. According to Hong Kong's Stamp Duty Ordinance, the transfer of shares (or Hong Kong securities) of a non-listed SPV requires both the buyer and seller to pay a 0.1% ad valorem stamp duty (Chapter 117 of the Stamp Duty Ordinance). When fund shares are transferred in token form, the on-chain transfer of tokens may constitute a "transfer of Hong Kong securities" in a legal sense, and is subject to a 0.1% stamp duty. Furthermore, if the relevant fund meets the applicable conditions of the Uniform Fund Exemption Scheme (UFE), profits derived from eligible transactions may be exempt from profits tax, thereby achieving tax optimization.

  • Path Three (Debt-Backed Tokens):

The tax treatment of this path involves the characterization of interest income at the investor level. Taking entertainment-related RWA projects as an example, the dividends received by investors holding concert revenue rights tokens are usually defined as interest income for tax purposes. If the investor is a local Hong Kong institution, it is necessary to assess whether the interest is derived from Hong Kong to determine whether it is subject to profits tax; if the investor is an individual, under the current Hong Kong tax system, personal interest income is generally not taxed.

IV. Behind the RWA Craze: Policy Focus and Compliance Implications

(I) Policy Focus

RWA does not exist in a vacuum; its prosperity is inseparable from the impetus of macro policies.

To understand the current surge in RWA issuance in Hong Kong, it's essential to examine it within the broader context of regulatory differences between mainland China and Hong Kong, and the upgrading of global financial infrastructure.

1. Mainland China: Strictly Prohibited Domestically, Strictly Regulated Overseas

Mainland China has consistently maintained clear regulatory red lines regarding the issuance, trading, and financing activities of virtual currencies. Policy focus is on empowering the technology industry and promoting the widespread adoption of the digital yuan (CBDC). The "Notice on Further Preventing and Handling Risks Related to Virtual Currencies," issued on February 6, 2026, further clarified the regulatory principles for RWAs: Activities involving the tokenization of real-world assets within mainland China, as well as the provision of related intermediary and information technology services, involving suspected illegal issuance of tokens, unauthorized public offerings of securities, illegal operation of securities and futures businesses, illegal fundraising, and other illegal financial activities, are prohibited; exceptions are made for related business activities conducted based on specific financial infrastructure with the approval of the competent authorities in accordance with laws and regulations.

... Subsequently, the China Securities Regulatory Commission (CSRC) issued the "Regulatory Guidelines on the Issuance of Asset-Backed Securities Tokens Overseas by Domestic Assets" (CSRC Announcement [2026] No. 1), establishing a dedicated regulatory framework for the issuance of tokens overseas by domestic assets in the form of securitization. This arrangement reflects the regulatory authorities' policy approach of "strict prohibition domestically, strict regulation overseas," prohibiting RWA activities domestically while also allowing for compliant overseas transfer of domestic assets.

2. Hong Kong: A Regulatory Testing Ground for Financial Innovation

Since the release of the "Policy Declaration on the Development of Virtual Assets in Hong Kong" in 2022, Hong Kong has established a relatively clear and favorable RWA policy environment globally. In November 2023, the Hong Kong Securities and Futures Commission (SFC) issued the "Circular on Intermediaries Engaging in Activities Related to Tokenized Securities" and the "Circular on Tokenized SFC-Approved Investment Products," laying the foundation for the regulatory tone of RWA in Hong Kong.

... In 2024, the Hong Kong Monetary Authority (HKMA) launched the Ensemble Sandbox project, focusing on the application testing of tokenized currencies in inter-institutional RWA settlement scenarios, and promoting the construction of financial infrastructure for the interoperability of tokenized currencies and assets in Hong Kong. In August 2025, the Stablecoin Ordinance officially came into effect, establishing a special licensing system for issuers of stablecoins pegged to fiat currencies. Stablecoins provide a compliant pricing anchor and payment tool for RWA transactions. This ordinance is conducive to further removing intermediate obstacles to the tokenization of real-world assets.

(II) Compliance Implications for RWAs in Hong Kong

Faced with the financing opportunities brought by RWAs, project teams issuing RWAs in Hong Kong cannot stop at narrative enthusiasm; they also need to simultaneously build a complete compliance framework.

General compliance aspects: First, the authenticity and independence of the asset structure. In RWA designs related to entertainment revenue rights or real estate, if the issuer promises fixed returns under the guise of tokens but fails to achieve genuine asset segregation at the underlying level, it is easily identified as illegal fundraising or an unauthorized collective investment scheme under the Hong Kong regulatory framework. Therefore, an independent third-party asset custodian can be introduced to reduce the risks of illegal activities such as fund pooling and rigid redemption. Secondly, there are strict on-chain anti-money laundering constraints. To meet anti-money laundering requirements, an executable mechanism for customer due diligence, wallet address screening, transaction monitoring, and abnormal activity identification needs to be established. Tax compliance: Firstly, the attributes of tokens affect tax classification, requiring reasonable tax planning. When reviewing token transactions, the Hong Kong Inland Revenue Department may penetrate the technical shell of smart contracts to directly examine their economic substance. Whether it is "property rights," "equity," or "revenue distribution rights (debts)" will affect tax classification and the application of preferential policies. Secondly, there are reporting obligations required by cross-border tax information exchange mechanisms. Under Hong Kong's CRS (Global Financial Account Automatic Exchange Standard) and CARF (Crypto Asset Reporting Framework), eligible financial institutions/crypto asset service providers are required to fulfill their customer due diligence and information reporting obligations. Thirdly, there is the compliance relief for cross-border withholding tax. When cash flows from mainland entertainment assets or real estate projects are remitted to a Hong Kong SPV, cross-border withholding tax is a significant factor affecting investor returns. Therefore, project owners should proactively assess whether they can apply the preferential tax rates under the "Arrangement between the Mainland and the Hong Kong Special Administrative Region for the Avoidance of Double Taxation and the Prevention of Fiscal Evasion with Respect to Taxes on Income"—for example, striving to reduce the withholding tax on dividends from 10% to 5%, or the withholding tax on royalties to 7%. However, applying preferential tax rates requires the Hong Kong SPV to plan ahead, obtain a Certificate of Resident Enterprise (CoR), and meet the strict substantive determination standards of the Mainland tax authorities regarding the "beneficial owner" status. V. Conclusion For mainland companies holding high-quality underlying assets, issuing RWAs in Hong Kong provides a new path to expand financing channels and access global liquidity. However, the real key lies not in whether the real assets can be "on-chained," but in whether a robust implementation framework can be established across legal, tax, and commercial arrangements. Hong Kong has already built the necessary infrastructure and regulatory environment. In this burgeoning wave of RWAs, the real winners will be those who can navigate the complexities of compliance.

data.title: Wu Jiezhuang spearheads a joint effort between China and South Korea to break the deadlock in Web3.
data.description: On March 23, the "Hong Kong-Korea Web3 Policy Promotion Alliance" was officially established. This is not a conventional regional forum, but rather a cross-regional policy collaboration platform promoted by the private sector. The alliance was jointly initiated by Hong Kong Legislative Council member Ng Kit-chung and South Korean National Assembly member and initiator of the Digital Asset Basic Law bill, Min Byung-deok. When Hong Kong's financial system capabilities begin to connect with South Korea's technology and market dynamism, this cooperation, driven by key policy participants from both regions, may impact not only Web3 or AI itself, but also the underlying structure of digital asset operations. Complementary Advantages For a long time, while the Asian Web3 market has been active, it has generally exhibited a relatively fragmented development. Hong Kong, relying on its mature financial system, continues to advance its compliance and regulatory framework, with its institutional foundation gradually improving from virtual asset licensing to the soon-to-be-launched stablecoin issuance mechanism. South Korea, on the other hand, is more market- and technology-driven, having formed a highly active industrial ecosystem in terms of crypto asset adoption, trading activity, and blockchain and AI applications. Wu Jiezhuang pointed out that Hong Kong and South Korea have significant complementarity in the fields of Web3, artificial intelligence, and digital assets. Based on this, this cooperation does not stop at the directional level but directly focuses on several key lines: the coordinated development of blockchain and AI, the compliance path for digital assets, stablecoin mechanisms, and cross-border data flows. These issues themselves are the most restrictive and also the most amplifying core aspects of the current digital economy. As these directions gradually advance, the cooperation between the two regions is expected to shift from single-point collaborations to a more systematic approach involving cross-border pilot programs and mechanism alignment. From a broader perspective, this cooperation reflects the phased differentiation of the global digital asset regulatory system. The US and Europe, relying on their mature financial systems and regulatory experience, entered the rule-building phase earlier, gradually forming their own frameworks, from stablecoin regulation to overall system design. Asia is not absent. Whether it's Hong Kong's advancement of a virtual asset licensing system or South Korea's promotion of digital asset legislation, both are essentially building regulatory systems suited to their own market environments, only lagging behind Europe and the US in terms of timing. However, it is precisely this "time lag" that provides greater room for adjustment for developing regions. Hong Kong and South Korea represent two paths: "regulatory capacity" and "market vitality," respectively. When these two capabilities begin to converge, the significance extends beyond mere policy communication; it represents an attempt to construct a new combination. More specifically, this convergence also corresponds to a match of real-world needs—South Korea's vibrant industries and capital require clearer compliant pathways for absorption; while Hong Kong's well-developed financial infrastructure needs richer business scenarios and sources of liquidity. In this process, Hong Kong's role as a "super-connector" begins to move from concept to concrete reality: through institutional and channel design, connecting regional technology and capital to a broader international market. **Stage Shift** If we extend the timeframe, this collaboration corresponds to a change in the development stage of stablecoins. In the past few years, the core issue in the industry has focused on "who will issue stablecoins," with competition revolving around scale, liquidity, and credit. However, as the infrastructure matures, the question is shifting to the next stage: how do these "on-chain funds" enter the real economic system, and under what rules do they flow? Different regions are offering their own paths. Singapore is exploring the on-chain transformation of traditional financial assets through Project Guardian; Europe is unifying regulatory standards through the MiCA framework; and the United States is beginning to restrict the "deposit-like" nature of stablecoins, redefining their financial boundaries. While the paths differ, the goal is the same—stablecoins are shifting from "crypto market tools" to "financial infrastructure." Against this backdrop, the cooperation between Hong Kong and South Korea will gradually manifest in more specific capital flow structures. Traditional cross-border capital flows rely on a multi-layered intermediary system for clearing and settlement, resulting in complex and costly processes. However, when stablecoins are integrated with regulatory frameworks, some capital flows may shift to on-chain processing, thereby reducing intermediaries and improving efficiency. This change will not replace the existing system in the short term, but it will gradually open up new pathways. Once these pathways emerge, the structure will begin to loosen. If regionally coordinated rules continue to advance, the result will not only be increased efficiency but also changes in capital pricing and settlement logic—structures previously concentrated in a single system may begin to decentralize. This does not mean that a particular system is replaced, but rather that capital flows across regions will have more channels to choose from. On the eve of the trial, another noteworthy detail is that this cooperation is not a traditional inter-state agreement, but rather unfolds in the form of grassroots policy coordination. This allows it to avoid reaching a highly unified institutional design at the outset, and instead gradually advance through more flexible pilot programs and collaborations, forming consensus from practice, and then influencing rule-making. The evolution of many financial infrastructures often follows this path. From this perspective, this alliance is more like a preliminary "structural experiment." It attempts to answer a longer-term question: as stablecoins begin to enter the real economy, and as the possibility of integration between different regulatory systems emerges, will the flow of funds gradually shift from a "single-center" to a "multi-regional parallel" model? If this trend proves true, then this seemingly regional cooperation today may affect not only Hong Kong or South Korea, but the entire operating system of digital assets.

data.title: SBF Pardon Odds Crash as Parents’ CNN Defense Backfires, Markets Turn Bearish
data.description:
The odds of a presidential pardon for Sam Bankman-Fried have slipped deeper into single digits after a high-profile CNN interview featuring his parents reignited controversy around the FTX collapse — and may have done more harm than good.

Parents challenge the narrative — and reignite scrutiny

On Polymarket, traders now assign just an 11% chance of a pardon in 2026, while Kalshi prices it even lower at 9%. Both slipped further following the March 21 interview, signaling growing skepticism that political intervention is coming.

The drop may appear small, but in prediction markets, momentum matters. And right now, sentiment is turning decisively against SBF.

In the interview, Joseph Bankman and Barbara Fried mounted a full-throated defense of their son, rejecting the fraud narrative that underpinned his conviction.

Timestamp: 6:00

Smerconish asks "What does Sam Bankman Fried's mother want to say to the president of the United States?"....then let's her ramble for the last 75 seconds of the interview unchallenged.

A completely shameless advert for SBF's pardon.https://t.co/qk4cHohkhY

— Pledditor (@Pledditor) March 21, 2026

They argued that Alameda Research simply borrowed funds like any other user on FTX, insisting the money was never misused and always backed by sufficient assets.

“The money was always there.”

But the defense cuts both ways. Their comments revived uncomfortable questions about their own involvement — including allegations in FTX’s bankruptcy filings that they received millions in cash transfers and a luxury Bahamas property tied to the exchange.

EXCLUSIVE Bankman-Fried's FTX, parents bought Bahamas property worth $121 mln https://t.co/zA6Gt91SEzpic.twitter.com/1S7khSW7iY

— Reuters (@Reuters) November 22, 2022

Political conspiracy claims escalate the stakes

Bankman-Fried's parents went further and framed the entire prosecution as politically motivated. Fried accused the Biden administration of trying to “destroy crypto,” while the family’s legal team is now pushing to overturn the conviction and even replace Judge Lewis Kaplan, citing alleged bias.

Was the judge biased?

FTX founder Sam Bankman-Fried has formally appealed his fraud conviction, requesting a new trial and accusing the judge overseeing his case of being unfairly biased against him.

The 102-page appeal was filed Friday afternoon.https://t.co/oIdn2ZyRrjpic.twitter.com/iHahdTTyZs

— CoinDesk (@CoinDesk) September 16, 2024

The strategy marks a dramatic pivot: from legal defense to outright political confrontation. In the most striking moment, Fried made a direct plea to Donald Trump, arguing that her son could be an “asset” to the country if freed.

But if the goal was to boost pardon prospects, the market reaction suggests the opposite.

Even within crypto-friendly circles, support for SBF appears to be dissipating. Senator Cynthia Lummis openly warned against clemency, while Trump himself has reportedly indicated he would not grant a pardon.

🚨 WHITE HOUSE UPDATE:

President Trump has clearly refused to grant a pardon to Sam Bankman-Fried—no clemency for the FTX founder! pic.twitter.com/i52avDl9xZ

— Crypto Aman (@cryptoamanclub) February 25, 2026

Behind the scenes, the family has been lobbying figures close to Trump since 2025 — but so far, it hasn’t moved the needle.

Prediction markets are often described as real-time truth machines — and their message here is stark.

At just 9–11% odds, traders are effectively pricing in near-zero political will to rescue SBF. To put it in perspective, markets currently see a far higher likelihood of a U.S.-Iran ceasefire than a Bankman-Fried pardon.

For a figure once at the center of crypto’s rise, the shift is dramatic.

From crypto king to political long shot

The interview was meant to reshape the narrative. Instead, it may have reinforced doubts — not just about the case, but about the feasibility of a last-minute political lifeline.

As appeals continue and lobbying efforts intensify, one thing is clear:

In the eyes of the market, Sam Bankman-Fried’s chances of a pardon are fading fast — and even a media blitz may not be enough to reverse the trend.

data.title: Who exactly are Binance and OKX's enemies?
data.description:
Author: Punk (2898) Source: X, @punk2898

First conclusion: Binance and OKX's biggest enemies have never been each other; they may even be a community of shared interests.

Recently, Binance and OKX have been competing with each other in various public occasions. On CT, people are comparing who is stronger and who has been surpassed by whom every day. The BD, operations, and KOLs of both companies are secretly competing. But to put it bluntly—these two companies are focusing on each other, which is a typical "competition trap."

You focus on your biggest enemy, and as a result, you get stabbed in the back without even knowing where the blood came from.

Outline of this article:

Why can't Binance and OKX kill each other? Second-tier exchanges: Three ferocious beasts no one dares to ignore. Compliance is a double-edged sword: armor is also a shackle. US stock trading—the most terrifying battlefield shift. Campuses, altcoins, and Chinese communities—a slow, agonizing process. The endgame: the enemy of my enemy is my friend. I. Why can't Binance and OKX kill each other? Many people have this ingrained mindset—market share is limited; it will either be swallowed up by Binance or OKX. This logic sounds correct, but it's actually completely wrong. Look at the current situation of these two companies: Binance has received the "endorsement" of the US government (CZ has finished his jail time and moved on), and has obtained compliance licenses in more than a dozen countries worldwide. OKX isn't far behind either—European payment license, Dubai VASP authorization, Singapore MPI, Australian AUSTRAC, and a federal registration license in the US. To put it bluntly, these two companies are too big to fail. The only thing that could bring them down is the risk inherent in their founders. However, CZ is currently keeping a very low profile, and Star is also focusing on product development; both are quite conservative. Therefore, the conclusion is simple: neither of them can completely destroy the other. Market share may fluctuate, but the overall landscape will remain the same. The question then arises—since neither can completely eliminate the other, who is eroding market share?

Second-tier exchanges: Three fierce beasts that no one dares to ignore

The answer is those second-tier exchanges that are eyeing us covetously

Data report from CoinDest, February 2026

Don't underestimate them just because they don't have the same level of influence as Binance and OKX

Don't be fooled by their lower profile compared to Binance and OKX

Large, but each player has carved out its own niche in a specific market. Bybit is the most competitive among the second-tier players, with the best refined operations. It boasts 70 million registered users and ranks among the top five globally in derivatives trading volume. Bybit is clever; it's not high-profile in PR, quietly making its mark. Look at its contract products, copy trading system, and wealth management products—their iteration speed is even faster than OKX's (the reasons will be explained later). Bitget has found its own unique path—US stock trading, and it's all in. They officially announced: "We are transforming into a full-asset exchange," which translates to trading US stocks. Partnering with Ondo Global Markets, they've listed a host of US stock tokenization assets, including Tesla, Nvidia, Apple, Microsoft, and Amazon, with 24/7 trading. Previously, could you trade Tesla on Binance or OKX? No. For the best liquidity in US stock tokenization trading, you had to go to Bitget. Why is Bitget the only option? In two words: compliance. Binance and OKX dare not touch this. Note: However, in the last week or two, Binance and OKX finally resolved their compliance issues and listed on US stock futures contracts. Gate has a relatively weak presence, but it did something that others were unwilling to do—listing all new coins immediately. Projects that couldn't be listed on Binance or OKX, or on Bybit or Bitget, could be listed on Gate immediately. In short, Gate replaced Matcha in its prime and Uniswap in the DeFi era—if you want to grab early alpha, you have to come to Gate. Everyone has their own place. "But these positions are slowly but surely cutting into Binance and OKX's flesh." III. Compliance: A Double-Edged Sword – Armor and Shackles. Why can second-tier exchanges erode the profits of first-tier exchanges? Because Binance and OKX chose compliance. Compliance is armor, making them too big to fail and attracting institutional funds. But compliance is also a constraint—if you want something, you have to give up a large part of it. When dealing with Binance and OKX, you'll notice a phenomenon: "Compliance is the first priority in everything." "Can this be done?"—First, ask about compliance. "Can that be launched?"—First, ask about compliance. "Can a new product be launched?"—First, ask about compliance. Compliance has become the top priority. This isn't wrong in itself; it's their foundation. But the problem is, the things compliant exchanges can't do are precisely the most profitable things. Who gets to take those profits? Second-tier exchanges

What's even more frustrating is that when problems arise, people don't blame the second-tier exchanges; they blame Binance and OKX instead.

Business competition is that ruthless.

IV. US Stock Trading—The Most Terrifying Battlefield Shift

What's the hottest sector in 2026? Everyone can see it—US stocks.

Altcoins are no longer traded, but everyone is still active. Where did they go?

The battleground is now dominated by two companies: Hyperliquid – the king of on-chain derivatives. Its daily trading volume reached $6.35 billion, weekly trading volume $15 billion, and active traders reached a record high of 231,000. It holds a 44% market share in on-chain perpetual contracts, pushing Aster's share down from 30.3% to 20.9%. What do these figures mean? This means a large number of users who should have been trading contracts on Binance and OKX have moved to on-chain. Bitget—the best centralized exchange for US stock trading—is a very clever "full-asset exchange" positioning. If you want to trade US stocks in the crypto world, besides Bitget, you can't find any other options, let alone liquidity (Binance and OKX are now catching up). Binance and OKX only listed a small number of US stocks in the first half of the day. Why? For compliance. So how can Binance fight back? There is one way—increase US stock trading in the Binance Wallet, with Aster as its biggest partner. But look at the promotional buzz in the market, it's very weak, nothing like Bitget's big-name marketing. This is a very clear signal: compliance is restricting their actions. And OKX? To be honest, they finally got investment from the NYSE's parent company and listed some US stock contracts a few days ago, but it's far from enough, and the trading volume is a bit low, plus they don't dare to promote it.

V. Campuses, Altcoins, and Chinese Communities—A Slow and Steady Cut

US stocks are just the largest part; second-tier exchanges are eroding much more than that.

Campus Ambassadors

I have to criticize this—promoting campus ambassadors to get college students to trade cryptocurrencies is morally wrong. Students don't have much money, and trading cryptocurrencies at this stage does more harm than good.

But from a profit perspective? Absolutely right

Are you promoting it while you're in school, or after graduation? The ROI is definitely higher while you're in school.

Young people develop usage habits, becoming lifelong users after graduation. Exchanges like Bybit and Bitget are doing this. Would Binance and OKX dare to do it? No. If they did, they'd be criticized, and compliance wouldn't allow it. So these future new users would be snatched away one by one by second-tier exchanges. Want to win them back? You can only wait until these people mature and then try to transfer them to exchanges with better liquidity and security. But that's difficult; user habits are hard to change once formed. New altcoin listings: This is another area that's been eroded the most. Binance and OKX can list fewer and fewer coins, and the review process is getting stricter. What if you can't list them? This share naturally went to Gate and other second-tier exchanges. Binance made a clever counterattack – Binance Alpha showcases and trades on-chain assets through its wallet, essentially reaching users without going through the formal listing process. This move certainly put second-tier exchanges in a bind. But what about OKX? Currently, it lacks an equivalent counterattack. It finally received investment from the NYSE's parent company, but the listing date is still uncertain. Regarding promotion in Chinese-speaking regions, OKX and Binance can only use online channels and cannot conduct large-scale on-the-ground promotions in Chinese-speaking areas. Second-tier exchanges don't have this concern; they can promote and operate as they see fit. All of this combined is a slow and steady approach. Each cut isn't fatal, but each one draws blood. That's why Binance and OKX have started focusing on ground promotion again in the past year. Why? Because they're being forced into a corner. VI. The Endgame: The Enemy of My Enemy is My Friend. At this point, the logic is clear: Binance and OKX's path is set—compliance, internationalization, and eventual IPO (Binance may list as a separate US company). You can think of it as a Coinbase story with greater potential. Coinbase can only serve US users and has little international competitiveness. Binance has a global user base, and OKX has the best product experience. Their future is clear, but the cost is also clear—compliance means giving up things—US stocks, rapid altcoin listings, campus promotions, and gray-area marketing—all of which have been taken over by second-tier exchanges. Therefore, Binance and OKX's biggest enemies have never been each other. "The real threat is Bybit's sophisticated operations eroding your contract users, Bitget's US stock trading stealing your active traders, Gate's rapid listings hijacking your Alpha coins, and Hyperliquid's on-chain derivatives bringing your users to the blockchain." And when these second-tier exchanges run into problems, the public criticism is still directed at Binance and OKX. Good deeds are theirs, bad deeds are yours. This is business warfare, a life-or-death struggle, where morality is irrelevant. The enemy of my enemy is my friend—Binance and OKX, instead of focusing on each other, should think about how to work together to deal with these challengers who are slowly but surely cutting their losses. "When the snipe and the clam fight, the fisherman benefits." — *Strategies of the Warring States*, Book 2, Yan.

data.title: AI companies haven't issued their own tokens, but they're all selling them like crazy.
data.description: Today I attended an exchange event at Fudan University about Web3 and AI. During the conversation, a rather interesting question suddenly popped into my head: What exactly are companies that make large model APIs, like Kimi and MiniMax, selling? On the surface, they seem to be selling model capabilities—question answering, generation, inference, search, and tool usage. However, if we set aside these embellished claims and focus solely on the most basic business operations, you'll find something very similar to Web3: Every time you invoke the model, the system deducts a portion of your tokens. Looking back at public blockchains at this point, you'll see that these two systems are actually quite similar. In the Web3 world, it's said that I sent a transaction that consumed 0.01 ETH. In the AI ​​world, it's said that I invoked a model once, consuming 100,000 tokens. The former sounds like the language of the blockchain world, while the latter sounds like the billing methods for cloud computing or SaaS products. But if you look at it one layer deeper, what they do is actually very similar: They both break down an underlying resource into the smallest units that can be computed, consumed, and settled, and then sell them to developers and users. From this perspective, I think what many AI companies are doing today and what many public blockchain projects have done in the past are actually similar in their business structure. They are both selling tokens. Of course, the "token" here doesn't mean that AI companies actually issue a freely tradable, exchange-listed, and price-manipulated coin like public blockchain projects. That's not what I mean. What I mean is that they are all selling a standardized unit of resource access. Essentially, they are all selling the right to access resources. When you use Kimi, you're not directly buying "an article" or "an answer." You're buying the model's ability to process text, the use of the context window, the consumption of the inference process, and the frequency and amount of API calls. The platform simply breaks down these abstract concepts into tokens and charges you based on the amount consumed. It's the same when you work on a public blockchain. You're not directly buying the words "transfer successful," but rather paying for the resources consumed by the network to complete a transaction, verification, sorting, and state update. The only difference is that the on-chain world calls this resource consumption "gas," and ultimately pays with native tokens like ETH and SOL. Therefore, AI companies and public blockchain projects, in a very fundamental sense, are indeed very similar: they don't directly sell results, but rather sell "the right to access underlying computing resources." They look alike, but are fundamentally different. However, this article doesn't stop here. Because the tokens sold by AI companies and those sold by public blockchain projects, although they look similar, are actually not the same thing. The core difference lies in their underlying power structures. The token sold to you by an AI company is essentially a unit of account within the platform. You deposit money, open an account, obtain an API key, and then consume your quota according to the platform's rules. What you possess is not a freely circulated, freely transferable asset that exists independently of the platform, but rather a right of use recognized by the platform. How can you understand it? It's more like in-game currency, or a cloud provider's backend access limit, or a balance in a membership system. This thing certainly has value because it can be exchanged for services; however, its value boundaries, usage rules, and price adjustments are basically controlled by the platform. But public blockchain tokens are different. ETH, SOL, and similar tokens are not just units of measurement within the system; they are also native assets of the network. They can be held, transferred, traded, pledged, mortgaged, and can exist independently of a specific call. One is platform pricing, the other is network pricing. Today, the price of AI company tokens is basically set by the companies themselves. What model you call, how much the input costs, how much the output costs, how much the long context costs, how much the tool call costs—all of these are pre-defined by the platform in the backend. Whether users accept it or not, it's essentially still platform pricing. However, the gas fees on public blockchains are not simply a matter of platforms posting price tags. On-chain fees are affected by network congestion, market supply and demand, user bidding, and protocol mechanisms. The billing logic of AI tokens is essentially enterprise pricing; the billing logic of public blockchain tokens is more closely related to joint pricing by the protocol and the market. AI, in turn, has taught Web3 a lesson. The most valuable aspect of this observation is not that "AI is very similar to Web3," but that it helps us re-understand an old question: Why do many Web3 projects ultimately fail, while the billing of AI company tokens seems naturally reasonable? The reason is simple. Because AI companies' tokens have very clear resource objects behind them, and very clear reasons for payment. When you use a model, you are truly consuming computing power. When you run long contexts, you are truly occupying window resources. When you do searches or make tool calls, you are truly increasing platform costs. Every additional call you make increases the platform's marginal cost. Therefore, it's logical for the platform to divide this consumption into tokens and then charge based on the tokens. Users understand immediately: the money I spend corresponds to the resources I actually consume. However, the problem with many past Web3 projects wasn't that they were called tokens, but that they lacked strong real-world consumption scenarios. Many projects, when discussing their business models, didn't first consider why users would continue to use and pay; instead, they focused on "how to build up the token." First, demonstrate the usefulness, then talk about the token. I think the biggest inspiration AI companies can offer Web3 entrepreneurs lies here: First, clarify one thing: Who are users actually paying for? Let's solidify one more thing: Why do users repeatedly pay? Then look further: Does this business truly involve a resource consumption that can be segmented, measured, and settled? Instead of immediately asking how to issue tokens, how to list on exchanges, and how to manage market capitalization. Not everything is worth putting on-chain. Because many businesses are inherently more efficient and have clearer contractual relationships on centralized platforms, there's no need to force them into an on-chain architecture. Not all units of measurement are worth trading. Many units are only suitable for internal settlement; allowing them to trade freely in the market can distort their original usage logic. Often, the best token isn't the one with the fastest price increase, but rather the one that doesn't require constant price monitoring yet is continuously consumed, settled, and repurchased in real-world business operations. In summary, returning to today's topic: "AI companies haven't issued their own tokens, but they're selling them like crazy." What AI companies are selling isn't cryptocurrency in the traditional sense. What they're selling is a token that has been corporatized, productized, and contractualized. The Web3 world shouldn't just be selling a coin whose value can fluctuate. Its true value should lie in the native pricing power over a resource, a network capability, or a right to change state. Therefore, let's stop thinking about issuing tokens. First, figure out what you're actually selling. Are you selling a story or a resource? Are you selling imagination or the right to use it? Are you selling a financial illusion or a real, repeatedly consumed, repeatedly priced, and repeatedly repurchased underlying capability? This may be the most valuable reminder that AI brings to Web3.

data.title: What will become of the US-Iran conflict? Where will the market go from here?
data.description:
Tao Zhu, Golden Finance

According to a report by the New York Times on March 29, local time, two US military officials revealed that hundreds of US special operations personnel, including Army Rangers and Navy SEALs, have arrived in the Middle East to "provide more options" for US military action against Iran. The report stated that these special operations personnel have not yet been assigned specific missions, but as professional ground combat troops, they may participate in missions related to the Strait of Hormuz, Kharg Island, or Iranian nuclear facilities.

What will the future situation in Iran be like? What will the market trend be?

I. Recent Developments in the Middle East Conflict

1. "Island Seizure" and Troop Increase

On March 29, local time, Trump stated that he hoped to "seize Iran's oil resources" and might even occupy Kharg Island, a crucial export hub.

"To be honest, my favorite thing to do is take oil from Iran, but some stupid people in the United States say, 'Why do you do that?' But they are stupid people." Kharg Island was previously little known, but it gained notoriety during this war due to Trump's threats. Kharg Island has a total area of ​​only 20 square kilometers and is administered by the neighboring Bushehr province. It has a seaport and an airport.

In 2015, the terminal facilities on the island were operated by the National Iranian Oil Company. On March 13, the United States announced that it had bombed military facilities on Kharg Island as part of a planned war with Iran in 2026, but did not attack the island's oil infrastructure.

The US attack on this small but vital island in the northern Gulf is tantamount to striking at the lifeline of the Iranian economy. 90% of Iran's crude oil is exported through the terminals on this island, transported via pipelines. Very large crude carriers—capable of carrying up to 85 million gallons of crude oil—can sail directly to the island's long piers to load the oil.

Because the waters near the islands are deep enough, unlike the shallower waters off the coast of mainland Iran, oil tankers can dock and operate. As a terminal for Iranian oil exports, Kharg Island is one of the important sources of revenue for the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps. Corresponding to the aforementioned "island-seizing" plan, Trump is increasing troop deployments in Iran, and the possibility of amphibious landings cannot be ruled out. Trump has been strengthening the deployment of US troops in the region, and the Pentagon has ordered the deployment of 10,000 highly trained soldiers to seize and control the land. Approximately 3,500 soldiers arrived in the region last Friday, including about 2,200 Marines. Another 2,200 Marines are en route, while thousands of soldiers from the 82nd Airborne Division have also been ordered to the region. Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf stated that Iran will unleash its firepower on any US troops attempting to enter Iranian territory. "The enemy is publicly signaling for negotiations, but secretly planning a ground attack." 2. "15 Ceasefire Demands" Trump's 15-point peace plan has not been officially released, but leaked versions show that the document simply compiles all the demands the US and Israel have made to Iran over the years. This document reads more like surrender terms than a basis for negotiation. Key points include the US demanding that Iran commit to never developing nuclear weapons, not conducting uranium enrichment activities on Iranian territory, abandoning the support of "proxies," opening the Strait of Hormuz and ensuring it remains a "free sea," and limiting the number and range of ballistic missiles. In response, the US will lift all sanctions against Iran and assist it in developing civilian nuclear power projects. On March 30, Trump told reporters on Air Force One that Iran had "met most" of the 15 ceasefire demands previously made to Tehran by the US, although it is unclear whether the two sides are actually engaged in substantive negotiations. He stated, "They have met most of our demands. Why don't they do that? We will raise a few more demands." Publicly, Iran has explicitly rejected the list of 15 ceasefire conditions and countered with its own five preconditions—including complete control over the Strait of Hormuz. Over the weekend, officials from Pakistan, Saudi Arabia, and Turkey met in an attempt to find a way out of the conflict, but there are currently no signs of substantial progress. The Pakistani Foreign Minister, after meeting with his counterparts from various countries, stated, "Both Iran and the United States have expressed their trust in Pakistan and their willingness to host subsequent negotiations." However, neither side indicated they were prepared for direct dialogue. Iranian Foreign Ministry spokesman Baghae stated that the suggestions submitted to Iran by the United States through mediators were extreme and unreasonable. In an interview with the media that day, Baghae said that these suggestions involved Iran's fundamental rights and did not reflect goodwill or a serious diplomatic attitude. He also stated that Iran must rely on its own capabilities to ensure its security and will use all means to prevent further attacks. 3. Passage Through the Strait of Hormuz Trump stated on the 29th that Iran allowed 10 Pakistani-flagged oil tankers to pass through the Strait of Hormuz last week, bringing the total to 20. He told the Financial Times, "These tankers have already set sail and are heading straight through the middle of the strait." The Speaker of the Iranian Islamic Parliament, Ghalibaf, approved the decision. When asked about Iran's plan to charge tolls in the Strait of Hormuz, Trump said, "I need to confirm if that's true, but the United States can very quickly stop this action. We can do this in two minutes. We can do it so quickly you'll feel dizzy." Iran has effectively set up a "tollbooth" and begun charging fees. Foreign media, citing Lloyd's Register, a shipping data agency, reported that since the 13th of this month, several global shipping companies have submitted documents to the Iranian Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps containing detailed information on cargo, ship owners, destinations, and crew lists, and have obtained passage permits through the strait. Lloyd's Intelligence reports that at least two ships have already paid the toll in yuan. Iran has begun to institutionalize the toll system. The Iranian parliament is currently drafting legislation that includes a proposal to levy a toll of approximately $2 million per ship. Iran's semi-official Tasnim News Agency reported on the 27th that if this system is implemented, annual toll revenue could exceed $100 billion. Before the war, approximately 120 ships passed through the strait daily. II. Will the situation in Iran escalate? 1. Trump wants to seize Iran's enriched uranium stockpile. The Wall Street Journal reports that Trump is considering a plan that could involve US troops entering Iran to seize its enriched uranium stockpile. If implemented, this would mark a sharp escalation of the situation. Before the series of airstrikes launched by the US and Israel against Iran last June, it was estimated that Iran possessed over 400 kilograms of highly enriched uranium (60% concentration) and nearly 200 kilograms of fissile material (20% concentration), the latter of which could be rapidly enriched to weapons-grade uranium (90% concentration). Former US military officers and experts pointed out that if the US were to seize Iran's highly enriched uranium by force, it would be an extremely complex and high-risk task, potentially provoking Iranian retaliation. 2. Trump Will Be Caught in a War of Attrition According to the BBC, if the US and Iran fail to reach an agreement, Trump has few options left: he could declare victory, claiming that the US has destroyed Iran's military capabilities and the mission is complete, and that reopening the Strait of Hormuz is not his responsibility—this could trigger severe turmoil in global financial markets and shock his already disgruntled European, Asian, and Gulf allies. A wounded and angry Iranian regime still has considerable room to further pressure the world economy.

Furthermore, the US's move to increase troop presence in Iran suggests it is considering an amphibious landing operation. This may play right into Iran's hands, as Iran hopes to drag the US into a prolonged war of attrition.

3. Iran's Proxy Wars

Since the 1980s, Iranian leaders have been attempting to cultivate armed partners in Arab countries. Through Hezbollah in Lebanon, militias in Iraq and Syria, Palestinian armed groups in Gaza, and the Houthi rebels in Yemen, Iran has built a destabilizing network that allows it to project its influence while avoiding direct armed conflict with Israel and the US.

1) Hezbollah in Lebanon

On March 29, Hezbollah forces penetrated deep into northern Israel and are currently fighting in the Israeli-occupied territories of Galilee. Israeli media reported fierce clashes near Kiriatshmona and Metula, with several IDF outposts captured. The IDF rear command ordered an immediate large-scale evacuation, resulting in the complete paralysis of roads in the south.

3. Iran's Proxy Warfare

This is Hezbollah's deepest advance into Israeli-occupied territory in its history. The Iraqi Shiite militia is a military-political organization dominated by Lebanese Shiites. It is both a legal political party and one of the strongest non-state armed groups in the Middle East. It was formed in 1982 after Israel invaded southern Lebanon, with direct support from Iran and Syria, and was integrated from Lebanese Shiite militias. It made its public debut in 1985. 2) Iraqi Shiite Militia On March 29, the Iraqi Shiite militia group "Blood Guardians" launched hundreds of attacks on US targets in Iraq and the surrounding area using drones and rockets. The Iraqi Shiite militia is a paramilitary alliance in Iraq, predominantly Shiite. In 2018, it was incorporated into the government's security forces but retained its factional independence and pro-Iranian stance. The Iraqi Shiite militia has long been supported by the Iranian Revolutionary Guard's Quds Force and is distributed throughout Iraq and Syria.

3) Houthi Rebellion in Yemen

Houthi spokesman Yahya Sarreya said on the evening of the 28th that the group launched a second round of attacks on key targets in southern Israel using cruise missiles and drones. The Houthi military operation "will continue in the coming days" until the United States and Israel "cease their aggression."

The Houthis are an armed group that rose to prominence during Yemen's years-long civil war, becoming the country's most powerful political force. Due to its location near a crucial shipping route at the entrance to the Red Sea, it is able to disrupt international trade. The group has approximately 20,000 fighters and represents the Zaydi sect of Shia Islam. The Houthis initially gained widespread support from Yemen's Shia population in the early 2000s. The United States accuses Iran of arming, funding, and training the Houthis, but the Houthis deny being Iranian proxies, although they do state that there are common political interests between the two sides.

Iran has developed a low-cost, deniable, and highly flexible asymmetric warfare system by constructing a proxy network covering Lebanon, Iraq, Yemen, and other regions.

This system is not only difficult to destroy by traditional military means, but it can also continuously pressure opponents in conflicts and become an important bargaining chip in negotiations. With the Houthi rebels, Hezbollah, and other forces gradually intervening in the current conflict, the war is showing a trend of evolving from an "interstate conflict" to a "trans-regional proxy war," and the impact on the global energy and shipping system will be amplified accordingly. According to an analysis by The Guardian, the real significance of the Houthi rebels joining the war with Iran depends on whether they intend to launch a few missiles and drones at Israel from a distance, or whether they will use their geographical advantage of being close to the narrow Bab el-Mandeb Strait to effectively block shipping in the Red Sea, just as Iran has effectively blocked the Strait of Hormuz. If both channels are affected simultaneously, it will "severely impact global trade and oil supplies." 4. How long will it take for the war to end? Trump emphasized that the indirect negotiations between the US and Iran through a Pakistani "envoy" are progressing smoothly. Trump has set April 6 as the deadline for Iran to accept an agreement to end the war, otherwise the US will launch attacks on its energy sector. According to CBS News, the White House has privately informed allies that a diplomatic agreement between the US and Iran will take time. The US estimates that the intense fighting itself will continue for another two to four weeks. Iran is also skeptical of the US-Israel timetable for ending the fighting. The Israel Defense Forces previously stated that they expected the fighting to continue until Passover (the first week of early April). III. Subsequent Market Trends 1. Expectations of Interest Rate Hikes "Using the term '180-degree turn' to describe the recent changes in market expectations regarding central bank monetary policy is insufficient." Just a few weeks ago, the market was expecting the Federal Reserve to cut interest rates multiple times in 2026, but now it is clearly pricing in a possible rate hike this year. The latest data from the CME FedWatch Tool shows that the probability of the federal funds rate remaining above the current 3.50%-3.75% range by the end of this year is close to 30%, while the probability of a rate cut has fallen to 2.9%. This shift in expectations is primarily driven by inflation concerns stemming from the energy market. Since the escalation of tensions in the Middle East at the end of February, Brent crude oil prices have risen from around $70 per barrel to around $111 currently. Simultaneously, long-term yields on U.S. Treasury bonds have also risen sharply, with the 10-year Treasury yield rising from less than 4% a few weeks ago to approximately 4.40%. The newsletter *Crypto is Macro Now* points out: "Food and energy prices will unfortunately continue to rise and remain high for some time, at least until the Middle East shipping chaos is resolved. Even if a peace agreement is reached tomorrow (which is unlikely), it will take at least several months to ease the situation." 2. "The Fourth Oil Crisis" On March 30th, Zhang Chi, former chief strategist at Guojin Securities, wrote: Many investors feel that the current "US-Iran war" is similar to the "Russia-Ukraine war," but in reality, the comparability is low, especially in terms of the global energy landscape and economic impact. The "Russia-Ukraine war" can at most be considered a geopolitical event involving energy-producing countries, corresponding to a localized reduction in supply. Moreover, Russia, even without the US SWIFT system, still sells oil, so the impact is localized and short-lived. However, the core of the "US-Iran war" is the Strait of Hormuz. The energy supply impact extends not only to Iran but also to the entire Gulf region, which accounts for nearly 50% of energy reserves and more than one-third of energy production. I reiterate one point: the logic behind the evolution of the "US-Iran war" is not merely an "exchange of interests between nations," but also a "religious conflict." Therefore, there is a risk of a protracted war, a protracted war, and an escalation of the conflict. The Strait of Hormuz is Iran's "trump card," and it will not easily relinquish control—it can be understood that if Iran loses control of the Strait of Hormuz, it has lost the war! This is what I mean by the "fourth oil crisis" that will gradually emerge under this round of "protracted war." Zhang Chi states that the definition of the aforementioned fourth oil crisis—"a protracted US-Iran war" and "a long-term blockade of the Strait of Hormuz" leading to persistently high oil prices—is crucial and will directly determine the extent of its impact on future global inflation, economic conditions, and asset prices. Comparing the 1973 and 1978 oil crises, the shift in energy supply structure led to a prolonged period of high oil prices, sometimes lasting 2-3 years. In contrast, the energy price increases during the 1990 Gulf War and the 2003 Iraq War lasted no more than 6 months. Clearly, whether the current US-Iran war can bring about a qualitative change in global inflation, economic conditions, and asset prices will depend on whether its impact on high oil prices lasts long enough. My assessment is that such a risk exists. 3. Financial Market Crash On March 30th, the market decline triggered by the Iran war escalated into a complete collapse on Wall Street. Efforts by all parties to mediate a ceasefire and restore Middle Eastern oil supplies ultimately only resulted in further escalation of the situation, exacerbating market panic. The Nasdaq 100 index plunged 1.9% on Friday alone, entering correction territory; the S&P 500 index closed lower for the fifth consecutive week, marking its longest losing streak since 2022; bond prices fell, pushing the 30-year US Treasury yield close to 5%. BTC hit a recent low of $65,600 on March 28th and has since recovered somewhat, trading at $67,574.38, a 1.4% drop over the past seven days. On March 30th, Robert Kiyosaki, author of *Rich Dad Poor Dad*, posted on social media that the continued expansion of national debt and the increase in money supply will drive inflation upwards, putting continued depreciation pressure on dollar savings. He also believes that geopolitical conflicts may persist for a long time, providing upward support for oil prices and further exacerbating the inflationary environment.
